title: Why some cafés should refuse Wi-Fi
summary: Argue whether independent cafés should offer wireless internet by default or deliberately withhold it to preserve a particular kind of room.
topic: Whether independent cafés should refuse Wi-Fi as a deliberate design choice
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether independent cafés should offer free Wi-Fi as a matter of course, or whether some should deliberately refuse it to preserve conversation, table turnover, and a particular kind of public room. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one named city, café, or specific scene. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a small café gives up when it installs Wi-Fi for the convenience of remote workers is rarely something the owner…
Granted, the freelance economy has placed real strain on people who do not have an office and cannot afford a coworking…
Walking into a corner café in the San Telmo district of Buenos Aires, one notices, before the espresso has been ordered, that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: người làm việc từ xa
tốc độ luân chuyển bàn
chiếm chỗ trong quán cà phê
bàn được một người chiếm dụng
nền kinh tế làm việc tự do
không gian làm việc chung
uống chậm một ly cà phê (để ngồi lâu)
trò chuyện trong không gian xung quanh
úp mặt vào màn hình
không gian thứ ba (không phải nhà cũng không phải nơi làm việc)
đón một đám đông sử dụng máy tính xách tay
tỷ suất lợi nhuận hoạt động
chèn ép cuộc trò chuyện
âm thanh xã hội (của không gian)
chính sách không có Wi-Fi
nhịp điệu của một quán cà phê
không hoan nghênh việc nán lại
khách quen
mặc định chọn sự tiện lợi
tiếng rì rầm xung quanh
ma sát có chủ ý

sampleText:
There is a particular kind of café that a small neighbourhood needs and that does not survive on its own. It has, for the price of a coffee, a table available to whoever sits down for half an hour. People know each other. Conversations begin between strangers. The owner can name almost everyone in the room by Thursday. When such a place installs free Wi-Fi, none of these properties survives the year.

What a small café quietly gives up when it installs Wi-Fi for the convenience of remote workers is rarely something the owner has thought through in advance. The first effect is mechanical: a four-person table is occupied for six hours by one person and a laptop. The second is acoustic: typing replaces talking, and the soft, distributed murmur of conversation is replaced by the heavier silence of a roomful of screens. The third effect is social. Customers who used to come in for company stop coming, because the room no longer offers any.

Granted, the freelance economy has placed real strain on people who do not have an office and cannot afford a coworking space. A café that lets a translator or a designer work for the price of two coffees is doing a small piece of useful public service, and the moralising about laptops can become smug very quickly. Nobody is arguing that every café should be hostile to a person on a deadline.

Even so, the choice is not a moral one. It is an architectural one. A café designed around quiet, single-occupancy work needs different furniture, different lighting, and a different volume of ambient sound than a café designed around conversation. Trying to do both at once usually means doing neither well. What deliberate friction — no Wi-Fi, smaller tables, slightly noisier music — actually protects is the second kind of room, the kind that the freelance café has been quietly replacing across cities such as Buenos Aires, Lisbon, and Bangkok for fifteen years.

Admittedly, the no-Wi-Fi café is not the right model for every owner. Some neighbourhoods genuinely need cheap workspace more than they need a conversation room. The point is not that Wi-Fi is always wrong but that not having it should be allowed as a legitimate choice, rather than treated as eccentric or commercially suicidal. A café owner who keeps the room cabled for staff and uncovered for guests is making a defensible call about what the room is for.

What needs defending here is the idea that a café is not, by default, a free office. It is a public room with its own logic — and that logic is genuinely worth preserving in some specific places, even if it costs the owner the laptop crowd.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose a hidden cost
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a small café quietly gives up when it installs Wi-Fi for the convenience of remote workers is rarely something the owner has thought through in advance' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để chuyển trọng tâm từ hành động (lắp Wi-Fi) sang chi phí ẩn (điều bị mất đi). Trạng từ 'quietly' và 'rarely' tạo ra hiệu ứng đánh giá tinh tế.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a small café quietly gives up when it installs Wi-Fi for the convenience of remote workers is rarely something the owner has thought through in advance' uses a what-cleft to shift focus from the action (installing Wi-Fi) to the hidden cost (what is lost). The adverbs 'quietly' and 'rarely' create a subtle evaluative effect.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Stacked semicolon contrasts to build a three-effect description
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'typing replaces talking, and the soft, distributed murmur of conversation is replaced by the heavier silence of a roomful of screens' dùng song song giữa hai mệnh đề tương phản nhau, kết hợp dạng chủ động ('typing replaces talking') và dạng bị động ('murmur is replaced by silence') để tạo ra một sự đối chiếu phong phú về cảm giác. Cấu trúc này nén một thay đổi văn hóa lớn vào một câu duy nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'typing replaces talking, and the soft, distributed murmur of conversation is replaced by the heavier silence of a roomful of screens' parallels two contrasted clauses, combining active ('typing replaces talking') and passive ('murmur is replaced by silence') voice to produce a rich sensory contrast. The structure compresses a large cultural shift into a single sentence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một loại quán cà phê đặc biệt mà một khu phố nhỏ cần đến và không thể tự tồn tại. Nó có, với giá của một ly cà phê, một cái bàn sẵn sàng cho bất kỳ ai ngồi xuống trong nửa giờ. Mọi người biết nhau. Cuộc trò chuyện bắt đầu giữa những người lạ. Chủ quán có thể gọi tên gần như mọi người trong phòng vào ngày thứ Năm. Khi một nơi như vậy lắp đặt Wi-Fi miễn phí, không có tính chất nào trong số này sống sót qua năm.

Điều mà một quán cà phê nhỏ lặng lẽ từ bỏ khi lắp đặt Wi-Fi vì sự tiện lợi của người làm việc từ xa hiếm khi là điều mà chủ quán đã suy nghĩ kỹ trước. Hiệu ứng đầu tiên là cơ học: một cái bàn bốn người được chiếm dụng trong sáu giờ bởi một người và một chiếc máy tính xách tay. Hiệu ứng thứ hai là về âm thanh: tiếng gõ phím thay thế cho trò chuyện, và tiếng rì rầm mềm mại, lan tỏa của cuộc trò chuyện bị thay thế bởi sự im lặng nặng nề hơn của một căn phòng đầy màn hình. Hiệu ứng thứ ba là xã hội. Khách hàng từng đến vì sự bầu bạn dừng đến, vì căn phòng không còn cung cấp điều đó nữa.

Thừa nhận rằng, nền kinh tế làm việc tự do đã đặt áp lực thực sự lên những người không có văn phòng và không đủ khả năng chi trả không gian làm việc chung. Một quán cà phê cho phép một biên dịch viên hay một nhà thiết kế làm việc với giá hai ly cà phê đang làm một mẩu nhỏ dịch vụ công hữu ích, và việc đạo lý hóa về máy tính xách tay có thể trở nên tự mãn rất nhanh. Không ai đang lập luận rằng mỗi quán cà phê phải không hoan nghênh một người đang chạy hạn chót.

Dẫu vậy, sự lựa chọn không phải là về đạo đức. Đó là về kiến trúc. Một quán cà phê được thiết kế cho công việc yên tĩnh, một mình cần đồ nội thất khác, ánh sáng khác, và âm lượng âm thanh xung quanh khác so với một quán cà phê được thiết kế cho trò chuyện. Cố gắng làm cả hai cùng một lúc thường có nghĩa là không làm tốt cái nào. Điều mà ma sát có chủ ý — không Wi-Fi, bàn nhỏ hơn, nhạc hơi ồn hơn — thực sự bảo vệ là loại căn phòng thứ hai, loại mà quán cà phê dành cho người làm việc tự do đã âm thầm thay thế trên khắp các thành phố như Buenos Aires, Lisbon, và Bangkok trong mười lăm năm qua.

Thừa nhận rằng, quán cà phê không Wi-Fi không phải là mô hình đúng cho mọi chủ quán. Một số khu phố thực sự cần không gian làm việc giá rẻ hơn là cần một căn phòng trò chuyện. Điểm quan trọng không phải là Wi-Fi luôn sai mà là việc không có nó nên được phép như một lựa chọn hợp pháp, thay vì bị xem là kỳ quặc hay tự sát về mặt thương mại. Một chủ quán cà phê giữ phòng có dây mạng cho nhân viên và không có cho khách đang đưa ra một quyết định có thể bảo vệ được về việc căn phòng dùng để làm gì.

Điều cần được bảo vệ ở đây là ý tưởng rằng một quán cà phê không phải, theo mặc định, là một văn phòng miễn phí. Đó là một căn phòng công cộng với logic riêng của nó — và logic đó thực sự đáng được bảo tồn ở một số nơi cụ thể, ngay cả khi điều đó khiến chủ quán mất đi đám đông máy tính xách tay.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to highlight a hidden cost of installing Wi-Fi?
options: Rarely something the owner has thought through what a small café gives up Wi-Fi installs.
What a small café quietly gives up when it installs Wi-Fi for the convenience of remote workers is rarely something the owner has thought through in advance.
Quietly the owner thought through in advance what a small café gives up Wi-Fi installs is rarely something.
The owner has rarely thought through what a small café gives up quietly installs in advance Wi-Fi remote workers.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' places the surprising assessment ('rarely something the owner has thought through in advance') in the emphatic position.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the writer's evaluation delayed to the end.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'third place' mean in the sense used in urbanism?
options: A literal third building owned by a person, after their home and office
A public, informal social space — café, pub, library — that is neither home nor workplace
The third storey of a multi-storey building
A literal place ranked third in a competition
mcQuestions.explanation: In sociology, a 'third place' is a venue that supports informal, regular social life beyond the home (first place) and the workplace (second place) — exactly the kind of café the essay defends.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of the home as the first place and work as the second. What is missing from social life if there is no third?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'granted' correctly as a concession?
options: Granted, the freelance economy has placed real strain on people who do not have an office and cannot afford a coworking space.
The freelance economy granted real strain on people office have do not who.
On people who do not have an office granted the freelance economy placed real strain.
Placed real strain on people coworking space afford cannot the freelance economy granted who.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause cleanly opens a concession to the opposing argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a passive construction to describe the acoustic shift?
options: The soft, distributed murmur of conversation is replaced by the heavier silence of a roomful of screens.
Screens replace the murmur of conversation in a roomful soft distributed.
Replaces a roomful of screens the heavier silence soft distributed murmur of conversation.
Heavier silence of a roomful of screens replaces the soft murmur of conversation distributed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is replaced by' is the passive: 'the murmur of conversation' sits in subject position, foregrounding what is lost rather than what does the replacing.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence makes 'the murmur of conversation' the grammatical subject?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to nurse a single coffee' mean?
options: To deliver a single coffee to a person who is ill
To drink a single coffee very slowly so as to occupy a table for a long time
To work as a nurse in a café for a single day
To sip a coffee from a baby bottle
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To nurse a drink' means to drink it slowly, often with the implication of occupying a seat or extending one's stay.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'nurse' as 'take care of slowly' — what would that mean applied to a drink?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'the choice is not a moral one. It is an architectural one'?
options: The decision about Wi-Fi should be made by an architect, not a moralist.
Whether to install Wi-Fi is not about being good or bad; it is about what kind of room you are designing.
Moral choices and architectural choices are exactly the same in cafés.
All architectural decisions are inherently moral decisions.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's point is that this is not about right and wrong but about design coherence — different uses need different rooms, and trying to host both badly serves neither.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the essay contrasting — 'moral' against what kind of opposite?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'deliberate friction' mean in the essay?
options: A planned argument between café customers and staff
Design features intentionally chosen to make a particular use harder, so a different use is protected
The natural wear and tear caused by frequent customers
A friction between music genres played in the café
mcQuestions.explanation: Deliberate friction is design that makes one thing (long laptop sessions) harder so that something else (short visits, conversation) becomes possible.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'friction' do — make things easier or harder? And what is the friction protecting?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause to compress a description of the café type?
options: A café designed around quiet, single-occupancy work needs different furniture, different lighting, and a different volume of ambient sound than a café designed around conversation.
Designed around quiet single-occupancy work a café different furniture lighting different ambient sound volume different than designed around conversation a café needs.
Conversation designed around a café needs quiet single-occupancy work designed around a café than different furniture.
Different furniture lighting designed around quiet single-occupancy work conversation a café needs around.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'designed around quiet, single-occupancy work' and 'designed around conversation' are reduced past-participle clauses, each compressing a full definition of a café type into a noun phrase.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for past-participle phrases ('designed around …') that modify the noun 'café' twice.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the choice is not a moral one. It is an architectural one.
On the contrary, Wi-Fi must be banned everywhere.
Furthermore, all cafés are the same.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the concession (freelancers face real strain) and pivots to the writer's main move — reframing the choice as architectural rather than moral.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reframes the issue?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All cafés should ban Wi-Fi immediately.
All cafés should offer free Wi-Fi as a baseline expectation.
Some cafés should be allowed and respected for choosing to refuse Wi-Fi, because that choice protects a specific kind of public room.
Wi-Fi should be available in cafés only for paying members.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay does not call for a universal ban; it argues that the no-Wi-Fi café is a legitimate, defensible model worth protecting from default expectations.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the fifth paragraph for the writer's clearest qualifying statement.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly cafés with Wi-Fi are kind of soulless and we should bring back the old ones.
Wi-Fi cafés are basically just offices and that's pretty annoying for everyone else.
Default provision of wireless internet alters the social acoustics of a café in ways that are difficult to reverse once a regular laptop clientele has formed.
It's super obvious that some cafés should just be for talking, not for laptops, full stop.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('default provision', 'social acoustics', 'regular clientele') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal intensifiers.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'basically', 'pretty', 'super', 'full stop', and other colloquial markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'commercially suicidal' mean in the essay?
options: An action that physically endangers a business owner's life
A business choice that is widely assumed to guarantee the failure of the business
A decision made for ethical rather than commercial reasons
A merger between two struggling companies
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase is a metaphor: a 'commercially suicidal' choice is one widely believed to doom the business. The essay argues that the no-Wi-Fi choice is treated this way unfairly.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'suicidal' mean when applied not to a person but to a business decision?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: Customers who used to come in for company stop coming, because the room no longer offers any.
Used to come in for company who customers stop coming, because the room no longer offers any.
Stop coming customers who used to come in for company because the room no longer offers any.
The room no longer offers any customers stop coming who used to come in for company because.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who used to come in for company' is a defining relative clause specifying which customers the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the moralising about laptops can become smug very quickly' mean?
options: People who argue against laptops in cafés often come across as self-satisfied and superior, which weakens their position.
Laptops in cafés are morally neutral and should not be discussed.
Café owners enjoy lecturing customers who bring laptops.
Morality and laptops are completely unrelated subjects.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay is self-critical here: it warns that the anti-laptop position, taken too far, becomes morally superior and unattractive — a real risk the writer wants to acknowledge.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'smug' mean, and why would the writer warn against becoming it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay by stating what is actually being defended?
options: In conclusion, cafés are important to neighbourhoods.
Therefore, all Wi-Fi must be removed from all cafés immediately.

What needs defending here is the idea that a café is not, by default, a free office. It is a public room with its own logic — and that logic is genuinely worth preserving in some specific places, even if it costs the owner the laptop crowd.
To sum up, both Wi-Fi cafés and conversation cafés have their place in cities.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C states the essay's position precisely — defending the idea, accepting the cost, and locating where this matters — without slipping into bland summary.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option states exactly what the writer is defending and what they accept as the cost?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay argue is the three-stage effect of installing Wi-Fi in a small conversational café?
options: Higher prices, more staff, longer hours
A mechanical effect on table occupancy, an acoustic effect on the room's sound, and a social effect on who chooses to come in
Better service, better coffee, more customers
Public relations, marketing, expansion
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's second paragraph names exactly these three effects in order: mechanical (table occupancy), acoustic (typing replacing talking), and social (regulars stop coming).
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph two for the three effects listed in sequence.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a café, bar, library, or other public room you visit regularly. What design choices does the owner appear to have made — Wi-Fi, music, table size, lighting — and what kind of room do those choices add up to? If one of those choices were reversed tomorrow, what would change about who came in and what they did there?
